title: OOP: Classes & Objects
description: Build the foundation of object-oriented programming — defining classes with fields and methods, creating objects on the heap, and understanding reference semantics — exactly as tested in CSE110 exams.

sections.content: 🎓 Why OOP Classes Matter in CSE110 Exams

CSE110 exam questions about classes almost always test one of three things: **tracing object state** (what are the field values after a sequence of method calls?), **reference semantics** (does a method change the original object or a copy?), and **null / aliasing traps** (what happens when two variables point to the same object?). The material in this chapter is the direct prerequisite for every subsequent OOP chapter — inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation — so invest the time to understand it deeply rather than memorising syntax.

A **class** is a blueprint for creating objects. It defines two kinds of members: **fields** (also called instance variables) that store the state of each object, and **methods** that define the behaviours objects can perform. Each time you call `new ClassName()`, Java allocates a fresh block of memory on the **heap** and fills the fields with their default values (0, false, null) before executing the constructor.

sections.title: Full Class Anatomy — BankAccount Example

sections.content: 📦 Objects on the Heap — Stack vs Heap

In Java, **primitive variables** (int, double, boolean, char) are stored directly on the **call stack** — the value lives in the variable itself. **Object references** are also stored on the stack, but they hold only a **memory address** pointing into the **heap** where the actual object data lives. This distinction — reference vs value — is the single most important concept for understanding Java's behaviour when passing arguments to methods or assigning one variable to another.

rows: Exists only for the duration of the method call
Exists until garbage collected (no more references)
Copies the primitive value OR copies the address
The object itself is NOT copied — both point to same heap object

sections.title: Visualising Stack vs Heap

sections.label: Exam Trap: Assignment Does NOT Copy Objects
sections.content: Writing `BankAccount b = a;` does **NOT** create a new BankAccount. It copies the reference (memory address). After this line, `a` and `b` are aliases — two variable names for **the same single object**. Any change through `b` is visible through `a`. This mistake accounts for a large number of lost marks on CSE110 trace questions. Always ask: *'Are these two variables pointing to the same heap object or different ones?'*

Inside any instance method or constructor, `this` is an **implicit reference to the current object** — the one on which the method was called. You need `this` explicitly in two situations: (1) when a parameter name **shadows** a field name (both have the same identifier), and (2) when you want to pass the current object as an argument to another method. You do **not** need `this` when there is no name conflict — Java resolves field access automatically — but many programmers use it for clarity.

sections.title: this Keyword — When You Need It and When You Don't

sections.content: Java is **strictly pass-by-value** — but for objects, the *value* being passed is a **copy of the reference** (the memory address). This means a method can modify the object that a reference points to (the heap object changes), but cannot make the caller's variable point to a different object (the local copy of the reference is discarded when the method returns). This distinction is subtle and appears on almost every OOP exam.

sections.title: Pass-by-Value with References — The Classic Exam Trap

sections.label: Subtle: Strings Look Like Value Types But Are Objects
sections.content: Strings are objects in Java, so `String s = "hello"; String t = s;` makes `t` an alias for the same String object. However, String is **immutable** — no method ever modifies a String object in place. Instead, operations like `s.toUpperCase()` return a **new** String. So even though `s` and `t` are aliases, calling `s = s.toUpperCase()` only reassigns the local variable `s`; `t` still refers to the original `"hello"`. This makes Strings *behave* like value types in most situations, which is a common source of exam confusion.

When an object is created with `new`, all its fields are automatically initialised to **default values** before the constructor runs. For reference-type fields (any class type, including String), the default is `null` — a reference that points to nothing. Calling any method on a null reference immediately throws a `NullPointerException` at runtime, which is Java's most common runtime error.

sections.title: null References and NullPointerException

sections.content: Every field and method you have written so far is an **instance member** — each object gets its own copy of instance fields, and instance methods implicitly receive `this`. In Chapter 10 you will study **static members**, which belong to the class itself (not any individual object). For now it is essential to understand the contrast: if `balance` is an instance field, `acc1.balance` and `acc2.balance` are completely independent. If `interestRate` were a static field, all accounts would share one copy.

rows: The class itself (shared by all objects)

sections.title: Exercise 1: Tracing Object State Through Method Calls
sections.explanation:
Let's trace step by step:

1. `Counter a = new Counter("A")` → a.label="A", a.count=0
2. `Counter b = new Counter("B")` → b.label="B", b.count=0
3. `a.increment()` → a.count=1
4. `a.increment()` → a.count=2
5. `b.add(5)` → b.count=5
6. `a.add(b.count)` → a.add(5) → a.count=2+5=7
7. `b.reset()` → b.count=0

**First print:** `a.toString()` → "A: 7", `b.toString()` → "B: 0"

8. `b.increment()` → b.count=1 (irrelevant to remaining output)
9. `Counter c = a` → c is an **alias** for the same object as a (no new object!)
10. `c.increment()` → increments the shared object → a.count=8 (and c.count=8 since they're the same)

**Second print:** `a.toString()` → "A: 8"

11. `c == a` compares references — they point to the exact same object → `true`

sections.title: Exercise 2: Reference Semantics Through a Method Call
sections.explanation:
**Line 1 — `doubleIt(b1)`:** passes a copy of b1's reference. Inside the method, `b.value *= 2` modifies the actual heap object that b1 points to. After the call, b1.value = 20. → prints **20**.

**Line 2 — `replaceIt(b2)`:** passes a copy of b2's reference into parameter `b`. Inside the method, `b = new Box(999)` makes the **local parameter** `b` point to a new Box. The original variable `b2` in main still points to its original Box(30). The heap object Box(999) has no references after the method returns and is garbage collected. → prints **30** (unchanged).

**Line 3 — `swap(b1, b2)`:** b1 currently has value 20, b2 has value 30. The swap method swaps the `value` **fields** inside the objects — it does NOT try to swap the references themselves. After: b1.value = 30, b2.value = 20. → prints **30 20**.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Spot the Field-Shadowing Bug
sections.error: Lines 6–7: Logical error. `width = width` and `height = height` assign each constructor parameter to itself. The instance fields `this.width` and `this.height` are never set — they keep their default values of 0.0. The area() method then returns 0.0 * 0.0 = 0.0 instead of 15.0.
sections.explanation:
When a constructor parameter has the same name as an instance field, the parameter **shadows** the field within the constructor scope. Writing `width = width` on both sides refers to the parameter twice — it is a no-op. The instance field is only reachable as `this.width`.

This is a **logical error** (not a compile-time error) because Java syntax allows `width = width`; the compiler does not flag it as suspicious. The bug only shows up at runtime when the object behaves incorrectly. Always use `this.field = parameter` in constructors where parameter names match field names.

sections.title: Exercise 4: NullPointerException at Runtime
sections.error: Line 11: Runtime error — NullPointerException. The field `name` is never assigned in the constructor, so it retains its default value of `null`. Calling `name.toUpperCase()` on a null reference throws NullPointerException at runtime.
sections.explanation:
A field of type String (or any object type) that is not initialised defaults to `null`. The constructor `Student(int grade)` sets `grade` but leaves `name` as null.

When `printInfo()` calls `name.toUpperCase()`, Java tries to look up the `toUpperCase` method on the object that `name` refers to — but `name` is null, so there is no object. Java throws **NullPointerException** at runtime.

The fix adds `name` as a constructor parameter and includes a null guard in `printInfo()` for defensive programming. This is a **runtime error** — the code compiles fine but crashes during execution.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Design a Student class with a Method to Compare Grades
sections.approach: Define the class with two fields. In hasBetterGrade, use 'this.grade' to compare with 'other.grade'. In toString, concatenate fields. In main, use an if-else on hasBetterGrade to decide which student to print.
sections.explanation:
**toString():** Java automatically calls `toString()` when an object is concatenated with a String or passed to `println`. By overriding it, `System.out.println(s1)` prints `"Alice (92)"` instead of a memory address.

**hasBetterGrade(Student other):** the parameter `other` is a reference to another Student object. `this.grade` is the calling object's grade; `other.grade` accesses the other student's field directly (since both are in the same class, field access is permitted — encapsulation rules about `private` haven't been applied yet).

**Design note:** In a real application you would declare fields `private` and provide getters — covered in Chapter 14 (Encapsulation).

items: **Class** = blueprint (fields + methods); **Object** = instance created with `new`, lives on the heap.
**Fields** are initialised to defaults when `new` is called: `0` for numeric types, `false` for boolean, `null` for all object/reference types.
**`this`** refers to the current object; required to disambiguate when a parameter name shadows a field name (e.g. `this.x = x`).
**Assignment `a = b`** for object types copies the reference (address), not the object. After this, `a` and `b` alias the same heap object.
**Pass-by-value with references:** a method can mutate the object a reference points to, but cannot make the caller's variable point to a different object.
**`NullPointerException`** occurs at runtime when you call a method or access a field on a `null` reference — the most common Java runtime error.
**`==` on objects** compares references (memory addresses), not contents. Two objects with identical field values are still `!=` unless they are the same object.
**`toString()`** is called automatically by `println` and string concatenation — override it in your classes for readable output.
